summary: Jeff Atwood shares a memorable maxim from the C2 Wiki about writing maintainable code: imagine the person who will maintain your code is a violent psychopath who knows where you live. While acknowledging this is dramatic, he argues that such shock tactics may be necessary to drive home the importance of code maintainability. He connects this to his broader belief that maintenance programming, though unglamorous, constitutes 99% of real-world coding work.
key_points: The 'violent psychopath' metaphor is a memorable way to emphasize writing maintainable code
Shock tactics can be effective for communicating important software development principles
Maintenance programming is not glamorous but represents the vast majority of coding work
Developers need to shift their mindset from writing clever code to writing readable code
money_quotes: Always code as if the person who ends up maintaining your code is a violent psychopath who knows where you live.
It may not be glamorous, but it's 99% of the coding work in this world.
key_insight: Writing maintainable code isn't about elegance or ego—it's about empathy for the future developer who will inherit your work, even if that developer is you.
